---
title: "Housing & Cost of Living"
description: "Inflation, house prices, affordability, and household debt — Canada and OECD peers"
---

How much it costs to live in Canada — and to buy a home — has become a defining
question of the decade. The charts below track consumer prices, house prices,
the gap between home prices and incomes, and how much debt households carry.
International comparisons use a broad group of 17 OECD peers so Canada's position
can be read in context.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import (
    CANADA_COLOR, PEER_COLOR, HIGHLIGHT_WIDTH, PEER_WIDTH,
    HIGHLIGHT_COUNTRY, PEER_COUNTRIES, get_data_date,
)
from pipeline.charts import peer_comparison_line, ranked_bar, single_line
```

## Where Canada Stands

A scorecard of where Canada ranks among the 17 peers on each measure (latest available year). Dots are ordered by rank, so **further right is more favourable for Canada** (lower-is-better measures are flipped accordingly). Canada is red, key comparators in colour; hover a dot for its value and rank.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import page_snapshot
page_snapshot("housing").show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Inflation (CPI Year-over-Year)

Canada's inflation rate, measured as the year-over-year change in the Consumer Price Index (all items). The grey band shows the Bank of Canada's 1–3% target range, with the 2% midpoint as a dashed line.

```{python}
#| echo: false
try:
    cpi = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
    # Filter to single "All-items" series (not sub-categories)
    if "product_group" in cpi.columns:
        cpi = cpi[cpi["product_group"] == "All-items"]
    cpi = cpi.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
    # Recompute YoY in case CSV has stale values from mixed product groups
    cpi["cpi_value"] = pd.to_numeric(cpi["cpi_value"], errors="coerce")
    cpi["inflation_yoy"] = cpi["cpi_value"].pct_change(periods=12) * 100
    cpi = cpi.dropna(subset=["inflation_yoy"])

    # Color bars: red above 3% (above target range), blue below 1% (below range), dark grey in range
    colors = []
    for v in cpi["inflation_yoy"]:
        if v > 3:
            colors.append(CANADA_COLOR)
        elif v < 1:
            colors.append("#1f77b4")
        else:
            colors.append("#555555")

    fig = go.Figure()

    # Target range band (1-3%)
    fig.add_hrect(y0=1, y1=3, fillcolor="#e0e0e0", opacity=0.4,
                  line_width=0, layer="below")

    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
        x=cpi["date"],
        y=cpi["inflation_yoy"],
        marker_color=colors,
        hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%b %Y}</b><br>Inflation: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>",
    ))

    # Bank of Canada 2% midpoint target — label outside plot area on right
    fig.add_hline(y=2, line_dash="dash", line_color="#888", opacity=0.7)

    # Default to most recent 1 year — compute y-range from visible data
    from datetime import timedelta
    import math
    latest = cpi["date"].max()
    one_year_ago = latest - timedelta(days=365)
    # Buffer x-axis: half month before start, one month after end
    x_start = one_year_ago - timedelta(days=15)
    x_end = latest + timedelta(days=31)

    visible = cpi[cpi["date"] >= one_year_ago]["inflation_yoy"]
    # Y-range: tight to data with padding for buttons at top
    y_min_val = visible.min()
    y_max_val = visible.max()
    # Start at 0 if all values are positive (typical), otherwise pad below
    y_min = 0 if y_min_val >= 0 else math.floor(y_min_val * 2) / 2 - 0.5
    # Ensure target band (up to 3%) is visible, plus extra room for buttons
    y_max = math.ceil(max(y_max_val, 3.5) * 2) / 2 + 1.0

    fig.update_layout(
        xaxis_title="",
        yaxis_title="Inflation (%)",
        plot_bgcolor="white",
        xaxis=dict(
            gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
            range=[x_start, x_end],
            tickformat="%b\n%Y",
            rangeselector=dict(
                buttons=[
                    dict(count=1, label="1Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                    dict(count=2, label="2Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                    dict(count=5, label="5Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                    dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                    dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                    dict(step="all", label="All"),
                ],
                x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01,
            ),
        ),
        yaxis=dict(
            gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
            range=[y_min, y_max],
        ),
        showlegend=False,
        margin=dict(b=100, t=40, r=80),
        annotations=[
            dict(
                text="2% target",
                xref="paper", yref="y",
                x=1.01, y=2,
                xanchor="left",
                showarrow=False, font=dict(size=11, color="#888"),
            ),
            dict(
                text=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 18-10-0004-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv')}",
                xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.15,
                showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"),
            ),
            dict(
                text="The Bank of Canada targets inflation of 2%, within a control range of 1–3%.",
                xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.22,
                showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"),
            ),
        ],
    )
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("CPI data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

```{=html}
<script>
// Auto-scale y-axis when CPI chart x-range changes.
// Uses polling because Plotly 3.x does NOT fire plotly_relayout for
// rangeselector button clicks.
// X-axis tick spacing is left entirely to Plotly's auto-tick logic.
(function() {
  function setup() {
    if (typeof Plotly === 'undefined') { setTimeout(setup, 500); return; }
    var plots = document.querySelectorAll('.js-plotly-plot');
    if (!plots.length) { setTimeout(setup, 500); return; }
    // Find the plot holding the CPI bar chart (it may not be the first on the page)
    var el = null, barIdx = -1;
    for (var p = 0; p < plots.length; p++) {
      if (!plots[p]._fullLayout || !plots[p].data) continue;
      for (var i = 0; i < plots[p].data.length; i++) {
        if (plots[p].data[i].type === 'bar') { el = plots[p]; barIdx = i; break; }
      }
      if (el) break;
    }
    if (!el) { setTimeout(setup, 500); return; }

    // Build lookup array from trace data
    var trace = (el._fullData && el._fullData[barIdx]) || el.data[barIdx];
    var rawX = trace.x, rawY = trace.y;
    var points = [];
    if (rawX && rawY) {
      for (var j = 0; j < rawX.length; j++) {
        var ms = new Date(rawX[j]).getTime();
        var yv = parseFloat(rawY[j]);
        if (!isNaN(ms) && !isNaN(yv)) points.push({ms: ms, y: yv});
      }
    }
    if (!points.length) return;
    console.log('[CPI] Ready: ' + points.length + ' points');

    // Initialize lastKey with current range so we DON'T fire on first poll
    var xr0 = el._fullLayout.xaxis.range;
    var lastKey = xr0 ? String(xr0[0]) + '|' + String(xr0[1]) : '';

    setInterval(function() {
      try {
        var xr = el._fullLayout.xaxis.range;
        if (!xr) return;
        var key = String(xr[0]) + '|' + String(xr[1]);
        if (key === lastKey) return;

        var xMin = typeof xr[0] === 'number' ? xr[0] : new Date(xr[0]).getTime();
        var xMax = typeof xr[1] === 'number' ? xr[1] : new Date(xr[1]).getTime();

        // Collect visible y-values
        var vis = [];
        for (var i = 0; i < points.length; i++) {
          if (points[i].ms >= xMin && points[i].ms <= xMax) vis.push(points[i].y);
        }
        if (!vis.length) return;

        var lo = Math.min.apply(null, vis);
        var hi = Math.max.apply(null, vis);
        var yMin = lo >= 0 ? 0 : Math.floor(lo * 2) / 2 - 0.5;
        var yMax = Math.ceil(Math.max(hi, 3.5) * 2) / 2 + 1.0;

        console.log('[CPI] y:[' + yMin + ',' + yMax + '] ' + vis.length + 'pts');

        // Update key BEFORE relayout to prevent re-triggering
        lastKey = key;
        Plotly.relayout(el, {'yaxis.range': [yMin, yMax]}).then(function() {
          // Capture post-relayout range to prevent loops
          var nr = el._fullLayout.xaxis.range;
          if (nr) lastKey = String(nr[0]) + '|' + String(nr[1]);
        });
      } catch(e) {
        console.error('[CPI]', e);
      }
    }, 500);
  }

  window.addEventListener('load', function() { setTimeout(setup, 2000); });
})();
</script>
```


## Real House Prices

Inflation-adjusted ("real") house price index, set to 100 in 2015. Because it
removes general inflation, it shows how house prices have moved relative to the
overall cost of living. Canada is shown in red against OECD peers.

```{python}
#| echo: false
path = "data/housing/oecd_house_price_real.csv"
if os.path.exists(path):
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "house_price_real",
        title="", yaxis_title="Real house price index (2015 = 100)",
        show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Analytical House Prices | Data as of: {get_data_date(path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.52,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Real house price data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Real House Price Growth Since 2015: Ranked

Each country's index equals 100 in 2015, so this ranks **how much real house prices have risen since 2015** — not which country's housing is most expensive today. A rebased index can't show price *levels*; it shows change from a common starting point.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(path):
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "house_price_real", "Real house prices vs 2015 (index, 2015 = 100)",
        f"Source: OECD Analytical House Prices | Data as of: {get_data_date(path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f} (2015=100)<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## House Prices Relative to Incomes

The price-to-income ratio divides house prices by household disposable income
(indexed to 2015 = 100). It is a standard affordability gauge: when the line
rises, homes are becoming more expensive relative to what households earn.

```{python}
#| echo: false
path2 = "data/housing/oecd_house_price_income.csv"
if os.path.exists(path2):
    df = pd.read_csv(path2)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "price_to_income",
        title="", yaxis_title="Price-to-income ratio (2015 = 100)",
        show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Analytical House Prices | Data as of: {get_data_date(path2)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.52,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Price-to-income data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Change in Price-to-Income Since 2015: Ranked

This series is also rebased to 2015 = 100, so it ranks the **change** in the price-to-income ratio since 2015 — i.e. how much affordability has shifted — not the level of the ratio across countries.

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(path2):
    df = pd.read_csv(path2)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "price_to_income", "Price-to-income vs 2015 (index, 2015 = 100)",
        f"Source: OECD Analytical House Prices | Data as of: {get_data_date(path2)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f} (2015=100)<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## New Housing Prices in Canada

Statistics Canada's New Housing Price Index tracks changes in builders' selling
prices of new homes (the "total" of house and land), monthly. This is a
Canada-specific series shown over time.

```{python}
#| echo: false
nhpi_path = "data/housing/statcan_nhpi.csv"
if os.path.exists(nhpi_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(nhpi_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "nhpi", title="",
                      yaxis_title="New Housing Price Index (Dec 2016 = 100)",
                      rangeslider=True,
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 18-10-0205-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(nhpi_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("New Housing Price Index not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Rent in Canada

The Consumer Price Index for rent — what renters pay over time. Renters are roughly a third of Canadian households, so rent is a central part of the cost-of-living picture that owner-focused house-price indices miss.

```{python}
#| echo: false
rent_path = "data/housing/statcan_rent_cpi.csv"
if os.path.exists(rent_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(rent_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "rent_index", title="",
                      yaxis_title="Rent CPI (2002 = 100)",
                      rangeslider=True,
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 18-10-0004-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(rent_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Rent data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Household Debt

Household debt as a percentage of net disposable income. A value of 180% means
households owe $1.80 for every dollar of annual after-tax income. It captures how
leveraged households are — relevant to both affordability and financial risk.

```{python}
#| echo: false
debt_path = "data/housing/oecd_household_debt.csv"
if os.path.exists(debt_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(debt_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "household_debt",
        title="", yaxis_title="% of net disposable income",
        show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Household Dashboard | Data as of: {get_data_date(debt_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.52,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Household debt data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest Household Debt: Ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(debt_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(debt_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "household_debt", "% of net disposable income",
        f"Source: OECD Household Dashboard | Data as of: {get_data_date(debt_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f}%<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Download Data

- [CPI / Inflation (CSV)](../data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 18-10-0004-01
- [Real House Price Index (CSV)](../data/housing/oecd_house_price_real.csv) — OECD Analytical House Prices
- [Price-to-Income Ratio (CSV)](../data/housing/oecd_house_price_income.csv) — OECD Analytical House Prices
- [New Housing Price Index (CSV)](../data/housing/statcan_nhpi.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 18-10-0205-01
- [Rent, CPI (CSV)](../data/housing/statcan_rent_cpi.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 18-10-0004-01
- [Household Debt (CSV)](../data/housing/oecd_household_debt.csv) — OECD Household Dashboard
